blocks.text: God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble.

Ps 46: The glory of Mount Zion, residence of the Lord. A song of Zion, like Pss 48; 76; 84; and 122. The first of a cluster of three psalms about the reigning in Jerusalem. A refrain (vv. 7,11) structures the poem.
Therefore we will not fear, though the earth should change, though the mountains shake in the heart of the sea;
though its waters roar and foam, though the mountains tremble with its tumult. Selah
1–3 : The psalmist trusts in Zion, the center of the world and residence of the , which remains unshaken though the surrounding earth “totters” (NRSV ) and plunges into pre‐creation chaos.
There is a river whose streams make glad the city of God, the holy habitation of the Most High.
God is in the midst of the city; it shall not be moved; God will help it when the morning dawns.
The nations are in an uproar, the kingdoms totter; he utters his voice, the earth melts.
The Lord of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our refuge. Selah

4–7 : In contrast to the tumultuous and dangerous waters in vv. 2–3, the calm river of v. 4 is controlled by God to provide water and fertility for the holy city (see Ps 36.5–9n.). The attacks of the rebellious nations in v. 6 function as a historical complement to the cosmic disaster in vv. 2–3. Yet God’s , thunder, defeats them.
Come, behold the works of the Lord; see what desolations he has brought on the earth.
He makes wars cease to the end of the earth; he breaks the bow, and shatters the spear; he burns the shields with fire.
9 : Rather than just defeating the enemies, as in most psalms, God ends war on .
“Be still, and know that I am God! I am exalted among the nations, I am exalted in the earth.”
10 : Stop what you are doing and embrace the peace the has achieved. Zion is the place where God’s victory is most visible and most appropriately praised.
The Lord of hosts is with us; the God of Jacob is our refuge.f Selah
